programs.name: Community Care Services Program (CCSP)
programs.what_it_does: Provides home and community-based Medicaid services to nursing home-eligible seniors and disabled individuals to help them remain in their homes. Includes personal care, homemaker services, and adult day care.[1]
programs.who_its_for: Nursing home-eligible seniors and disabled individuals enrolled in Medicaid.[1]
programs.benefit_value: Varies by assessed needs; covers 47 individual and group services including personal support and medical equipment.

programs.name: Home & Community-Based Services Program
programs.what_it_does: Provides 47 individual and group services to help older Georgians remain in their homes. Available to non-Medicaid-eligible seniors through state-funded support.[1]
programs.who_its_for: Older adults who are not Medicaid-eligible but need assistance to stay at home.[1]
programs.benefit_value: Up to 47 services including homemaker aid, respite care, and home modifications; value based on individual plan.

programs.name: Georgia PACE (Program of All-Inclusive Care for the Elderly)
programs.what_it_does: Delivers comprehensive medical and social services to frail seniors in community settings like adult day health centers. Includes primary care, hospitalization if needed, and all Medicare/Medicaid-covered services.
programs.who_its_for: Nursing facility-eligible seniors aged 55+ who can live safely in the community with support.
programs.benefit_value: Full capitation coverage of all needed health and long-term care services without copays.

programs.name: Georgia Medicare Savings Programs (QMB, SLMB, QI)
programs.what_it_does: Pays Medicare Part A and B premiums, deductibles, and copayments for eligible low-income seniors. Helps reduce out-of-pocket costs for Medicare-covered services.[1]
programs.who_its_for: Low-income seniors on Medicare meeting income and asset limits (administered via Medicaid).
programs.benefit_value: QMB covers all costs up to $130/month; SLMB pays Part B premium ($174.70/month in 2026); QI covers part.

programs.name: Senior Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program (Senior SNAP)
programs.what_it_does: Provides simplified SNAP benefits with streamlined eligibility for seniors with no earned income. Delivers monthly EBT funds for groceries to improve nutrition.[2]
programs.who_its_for: Seniors 60+ (or 62+ after Feb 2026) with no earned income and fixed income sources like SSI or retirement; income verified via matches.[2]
programs.benefit_value: Monthly SNAP allotment based on household size and income; average $200-300/person, no recertification burden.

programs.name: Low Income Home Energy Assistance Program (LIHEAP)
programs.what_it_does: Offers grants to help pay heating and cooling bills and address energy crises. Includes one-time payments to utility providers and weather-related repairs.[5]
programs.who_its_for: Low-income households including seniors meeting federal poverty guidelines.
programs.benefit_value: Average grant $300-500 per household annually; priority for seniors and disabled.

programs.name: Weatherization Assistance Program (WAP)
programs.what_it_does: Improves energy efficiency in low-income homes through insulation, sealing, and furnace repairs. Reduces energy costs and health risks from poor conditions.
programs.who_its_for: Low-income homeowners/renters including seniors; priority for 60+ and disabled.
programs.benefit_value: Free services valued at $5,000-8,000 per home; lifetime energy savings exceed costs.

programs.name: Georgia State Health Insurance Assistance Program (Georgia SHIP)
programs.what_it_does: Provides free, unbiased counseling on Medicare options, appeals, and extra help. Helps seniors select plans and understand coverage.[1]
programs.who_its_for: All Medicare beneficiaries, caregivers, and families in Georgia.[1]
programs.benefit_value: Free one-on-one counseling; average savings $500+ annually through optimal plan selection.

programs.name: Home-Delivered Meals
programs.what_it_does: Delivers nutritious meals to homebound seniors to promote health and independence. Often includes 5-7 meals/week with nutrition screening.[1]
programs.who_its_for: Homebound seniors 60+ unable to prepare meals, often low-income or with mobility issues.[1]
programs.benefit_value: Suggested $3/donation per meal; provides ~1,000 calories/day plus safety checks.

programs.name: Caregiver Programs (Respite/Out-of-Home Respite Care)
programs.what_it_does: Offers respite care, adult day programs, and training to support family caregivers. Includes education and short-term relief to prevent burnout.[1]
programs.who_its_for: Family caregivers of seniors, including kinship caregivers raising grandchildren.[1]
programs.benefit_value: Varies; respite up to 240 hours/year, adult day ~$50/day subsidized.

programs.name: Senior Community Service Employment Program (SCSEP)
programs.what_it_does: Provides part-time community service jobs and training for unemployed low-income seniors. Helps transition to unsubsidized employment.[1]
programs.benefit_value: Minimum wage part-time work (20 hrs/wk) plus training; ~$10,000/year income support.

programs.name: Elder Rights & Advocacy Programs (Legal Assistance)
programs.what_it_does: Provides legal services, advice, and representation for elder rights issues. Covers benefits, housing, abuse, and consumer problems.[1]
programs.benefit_value: Free legal aid; resolves issues worth thousands in benefits/housing.

programs.name: Long-Term Care Ombudsman Program
programs.what_it_does: Acts as independent advocates for nursing home and personal care home residents. Resolves complaints and improves care quality.[1]
programs.who_its_for: Residents of long-term care facilities and their families.[1]
programs.benefit_value: Free advocacy; resolves 80%+ complaints, enhancing resident rights and safety.
